title: What Does Prayer Mean to Me, Beyond the Rituals of Childhood?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for the adult spiritual life, separate from what you were taught
excerpt: A journal prompt set for the woman who grew up with prayer in one form and now has a complicated relationship with it. Separates the adult's real relationship with prayer from the version she inherited and asks what remains that is truly hers.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on the broad spiritual literature on prayer as a universal human practice across traditions.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are re-examining your spiritual life or when you feel pulled toward prayer but uncertain what it means to you as an adult.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the adult spiritual life. Many women have inherited a prayer practice from childhood and have a complex relationship with it in adulthood. These prompts sit in that complexity without requiring a resolution.
What is my current relationship with prayer?
Not what it should be. What it actually is right now, on an ordinary day.
What did prayer look like when I was a child?
Write the specific version. Who was there. What was said. What it felt like.
What of the childhood version do I want to keep?
Even if you have left the religion. Even if you disagree with much of it. What of that form held something real?
What does prayer mean to me now, defined in my own words?
It might be very different from what you grew up with. Write the definition that fits your actual life.
Is there one small practice I could build that fits this definition?
Not a whole spiritual life overhaul. One small practice that would fit into a week.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on broad spiritual literature that understands prayer as a universal human practice taking many forms across traditions, and on developmental psychology's account of how adults revise inherited beliefs.
